EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain occurred across northern portions of Stewart County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain made road conditions treacherous primarily in the northern half of the county.  A number of one-vehicle accidents occurred in Dover and out in the county, according to Stewart County Sheriff's Office.  Some of those included several accidents on Tenn. 120 and on Bumpus Mills Road.  The Dover Police Department worked a couple on the Dover Bridge and three in front of the Dollar Store.  At one point, at about 0800CST, city and county officials were stationed at either end of the Dover Bridge to caution motorists.

August finally marked the first substantial rainfall for the Central Tennessee Valley since we entered into the heart of the drought in early 2006. The month began rather uneventfully with a series of mainly dry cold fronts. The parade of cold fronts was stopped just after mid-month as Tropical Storm Fay approached Florida. No measurable rain was observed between the 13th and 23rd. The big rainmaker was Tropical Storm Fay, which brought between two and six inches of rain, the heaviest across Lincoln County.  For most of the Tennessee Valley, this was the first substantially above-normal rainfall month of 2008.||River base-flow actually increased as a result of Fay, the first sign of improvement in stream-flows from the drought. Deep groundwater also recovered to near average, and soil moisture reached near average levels for the first time in about two years.||Drought conditions improved to below severe drought /D2/ status in Lincoln and Moore Counties.

Thunderstorms developed in an air mass south of an east through west frontal boundary across Central East through Southeast Tennessee. The atmosphere just ahead of the front was moderately unstable and uncapped with steep lapse rates and 40-50 knots of shear. Most of the storm reports included hail ranging in size from a penny to as large as a quarter. however, baseball size hail was also reported right in the middle of the thunderstorm watch area across Southeast Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree was reported down on Big Spring Road and another tree was down on Miller Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure system moved northeast from Middle Tennessee into Southeast Kentucky with line of thunderstorms developing just ahead of front. Instability was low so convection was low topped. Most of the wind damage in this event was straight line in character. However, closer to surface low, the environment was more conducive to the development of rotating storms and an EF1 tornado touched down at Big Stone Gap in Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were reported down county wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure system moved northeast from Middle Tennessee into Southeast Kentucky with line of thunderstorms developing just ahead of front. Instability was low so convection was low topped. Most of the wind damage in this event was straight line in character. However, closer to surface low, the environment was more conducive to the development of rotating storms and an EF1 tornado touched down at Big Stone Gap in Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down county wide.

A strong surface high over the Appalachian mountains kept temperatures at or just below freezing across much of northern Alabama Tuesday morning.  At the same time, a strong southerly low level jet developed and lifted Gulf of Mexico moisture northward over this cold air.  This caused widespread light freezing rain and sleet to occur in portions of southern middle Tennessee. Most locations received a glaze to 1/10 of an inch of icing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sewanee Police Department reported ice on all roadways in the town.  One car slide into a ditch, but no other accidents had been reported.  Also icy roads were reported on Keith Springs Mountain in southern Franklin County.

There was an isolated severe weather for Dickson County, and a flooding event in Houston County on April 4, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two school school buses were stranded for a short period of time without children. One bus was on Lewis Branch Road, south of Tennessee Ridge. The other bus was on Rye Hollow Road, or about 5.6 miles southeast of Erin. Water was crossing the road on Highway 147 in western Houston County, State Route 46N, and also just north of State Route 13. In addition, the Spring Hill area had water across the road due to clogged culverts. Also, Buckeye Road had high water. Maple Street in Erin was impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although rainfall was not particularly heavy for the month, the rain that did occur was of a higher frequency, with rain every few days.  The heaviest rain events were on January 10th and 11th, when many locations saw over an inch of rainfall; and on the 31st, when in many spots the heaviest calendar day rainfall since last August occurred.||Overall totals for January still ranged from three to five inches in the Central Tennessee Valley. These totals still were below normal in all cases, so drought conditions remained in D4 (Exceptional Drought).

These totals still were below normal in all cases, so drought conditions remained in D4 (Exceptional Drought).

Finally some longer-term drought relief came to the Tennessee Valley in March.  Rainfall deficits for the year barely grew and rainfall amounts were almost near normal.  A significant cold front moved through on the 3rd and 4th and was the month���s biggest rainmaker in many areas.  But periodic rain, and even a little snow, continued to fall throughout the month, about every four days.  ||However, by March 20th, dry high pressure built into the Tennessee Valley and turned off the rainfall for most of the rest of the month, with only trace amounts beyond that.  ||Drought conditions improved approximately one category across the board. By the end of March, none of the WFO Huntsville Hydrologic Service area was in the D4 /Exceptional Drought/ category, though more than half of the area was still in the D3 /Extreme Drought/ category.

A powerful upper level low pressure system moved into the Midsouth during the evening hours of April 10, 2008 and continued moving through the area during the day on April 11, 2008. The system first produced a few severe storms during the early morning hours of April 10, 2008 over Northeast Arkansas as it approached the area. These storms quickly died off. However by the late afternoon and evening hours, redevelopment occurred across much of the Midsouth. These storms produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding. The storms continued into the overnight hours before dying off. Redevelopment then occurred across Northeast Mississippi during the afternoon hours of April 11, 2008 as the system departed the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed along the Gulf of Mexico and tracked northeastward into the Southeastern United States. An abundance of moisture was associated with the system. In addition, cold air was also in place over West Tennessee as a result heavy snow fell in association with the system. Snowfall totals of 3 to 7 inches were seen across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of six to seven inches fell across Munford, Atoka and surrounding areas. Numerous accidents were reported from the snow-covered roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system approached the Midsouth from Western Arkansas as a surface low tracked northeast from South Central Arkansas into the West Tennessee during the afternoon and early evening hours of March 3, 2008. Showers and thunderstorms developed over West Tennessee in association with the system. Some of the stronger storms produced flash flooding and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in the 1100 Block of White Station Road in East Memphis.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of four inches fell across Savannah and surrounding areas. Numerous accidents were reported from the snow-covered roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system moved into the Midsouth during the evening hours of April 10, 2008 and continued moving through the area during the day on April 11, 2008. The system first produced a few severe storms during the early morning hours of April 10, 2008 over Northeast Arkansas as it approached the area. These storms quickly died off. However by the late afternoon and evening hours, redevelopment occurred across much of the Midsouth. These storms produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding. The storms continued into the overnight hours before dying off. Redevelopment then occurred across Northeast Mississippi during the afternoon hours of April 11, 2008 as the system departed the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees and power lines countywide.

Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew trees down on Milnor Drive in Raleigh.

Some of the storms produced flash flooding, damaging winds, and large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved slightly, mainly during the first half of the month.  Areas of D3 (extreme) drought were reduced to D2 (moderate) over all of southern middle Tennessee.||Several locations, mainly northwest of the Cumberland Plateau, received above normal rainfall for the month.  Areawide, rainfall totals generally ranged from three to six inches, while normal is between four and five inches. The rain came to an abrupt end by mid-month and very little rain occurred during the second half of April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in association with an upper level disturbance that moved through the Midsouth during the late evening hours of May 31st. One thunderstorm quickly became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew numerous trees and power lines down across northeastern portions of Henry County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved over West Tennessee on February 17th, 2008. The system produced strong winds with gusts of 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huntingdon AWOS measured a wind gust of 36 knots. The wind produced severe damage to the new Carroll County Highway Department building being built on Highway 22 North. The wind blew down rafters on the partially built building as well as twisting some of the exterior walls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September turned out to be an extremely dry month.  High pressure dominated the weather most of the month, with the only substantive rainfall occurring in the first week of September.  Even then, the heaviest rain from that event occurred just west of the area.  Due to the very dry airmass, high temperatures were above normal, and low temperatures were below normal for most of the month.||Drought conditions were generally status-quo during the month.  Although there was still some residual improvement from Fay early in the month, this was wiped out by the end of September due to the resumption of dry conditions.  This left D2 /Severe Drought/ in Franklin County.

Very light winds, no cloud cover, and a high pressure system over the Tennessee Valley produced an optimal situation for strong radiational cooling. With cold and dry air in place over the area behind a departed cold front, temperatures dropped below freezing for several hours across southern middle Tennessee as a result. Temperatures ranged from 24 or 25 degrees in the coldest locations to around 31 degrees in other locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Countywide temperatures dropped to or below 32 degrees for more than 3 hours.  The COOP site at the Fayetteville Water Plant dropped to 27 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Squall line type thunderstorms, with some bow echoes, occurred on January, 10. Isolated supercells spawned a tornado in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported several creeks were overflowing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air behind a strong storm system changed rain over to snow late Sunday night into Monday across portions of southern middle Tennessee. One to 2 inches of snow fell across Franklin county through the morning on Monday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one half of an inch to 2 inches of snowfall accumulated through the morning on Monday in Franklin county, Tennessee. Higher elevations received around 2 inches of snowfall.  The COOP site in Sewanee recorded exactly 2 inches of snowfall. At lower elevations, around one half of an inch of snowfall occurred.

A strong cold front pushed into extreme Northwest Tennessee during the early morning hours of December 15th, 2008. Very shallow arctic air filtered into the region behind the front. Rain changed over to freezing rain and sleet. Ice and sleet accumulations occurred with amounts ranging from around a half inch up to 2 inches. The ice accumulations made roads hazardous to travel and many accidents occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain switched to sleet. Minor ice accumulations occurred while up to an inch of sleet accumulated on the ground across Obion County. One death occurred from a traffic accident which occurred on slick roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance tracked through the area during the early morning hours triggering isolated severe thunderstorms with it.  Storm reports were primarily wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported several trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds countywide.  A barn in Cleveland was also damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across portions of West Tennessee during the morning on January 10, 2008. As the front lifted north, surface low pressure tracked along the Tennessee-Mississippi border during the afternoon hours. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the system. Some storms became severe producing flash flooding, large hail, and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding along Highway 70/79 between Milan and the Gibson/Carroll County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through West Tennessee during the late afternoon  into the early evening hours. A few severe storms developed ahead of the front. The storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds caused damage to several structures across Eastern Henry County. One business suffered minor damage in Paris while seven homes were damaged along Poplar Grove Road and in the Old Union Loop area. Numerous trees and power lines were also knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved over West Tennessee during the evening hours of January 25th through the morning hours of January 26th. Freezing rain or drizzle fell over the area as a result with some ice accumulation occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hundredth of an inch of ice accumulated across Chester County during the early morning hours of January 26, 2008. Numerous accidents were reported county-wide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of January 29, 2008, severe thunderstorms occurred ahead of an approaching cold front. Once the thunderstorms moved out of the area, strong gradient winds took hold and produced some non-thunderstorm wind damage across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured 67 mph at the Putnam County EOC. A warehouse on Whitney Avenue had part of its roof blown away. The warehouse is about 3 blocks west of the EOC.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper low pressure system in addition to a cold front moved into West Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of January 8, 2008. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding and isolated tornadoes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees in Dresden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient brought non-thunderstorm damaging winds to the higher elevations of east Tennessee on the 17th.  Strong winds in the valley was responsible for one death in Loudon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Knoxville News Sentinel newspaper reported a 63 year old man died when  a tree on a hillside was pushed by strong winds and fell on his vehicle as he was traveling west on Vonroe Road in Loudon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought continued to affect areas in the eastern part of Middle Tennessee in February, 2008.

The Super Severe Weather Outbreak on Feb. 5, 2008 produced supercelluar thunderstorms, well in advance of a multicell line of thunderstorms. The whole episode lasted about 6 hours. This occurred ironically while many states, including Tennessee, were participating in the Super Tuesday Primary Election. Fortunately, polls had already closed in the mid state when these tornadoes struck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observer reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper low pressure system in addition to a cold front moved into West Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of January 8, 2008. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along Highway 88 about 4 miles west-northwest of Halls. A mobile home was shifted from its foundation and destroyed. The tornado continued east-northeast and crossed Double Bridges Road where it caused significant roof damage to one home and destroyed an outbuilding of another home. The tornado continued across Mill Creek Road to Church Road where three homes were damaged. The roof was blown off of one home and completely destroyed the kitchen area. The other two homes suffered roof damage, fence damage and a collapsed garage. The tornado continues east-northeast and reached its maximum intensity as it moved across Highway 51 in the Poplar Grove Community. The damage was rated as a strong EF1 tornado with winds of 110 mph. There was significant structural damage to one home with the complete loss of the roof. The house was also shifted off its foundation. A machine shed was also destroyed. Many trees were snapped and uprooted along the entire path of the tornado. In addition, several power lines were knocked down. The tornado then moved into Dyer County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper low pressure system in addition to a cold front moved into West Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of January 8, 2008. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado continued from Lauderdale County into Dyer County crossing Highway 210 where trees were snapped and uprooted. The tornado then crossed Holmes Road near the Fowlkes Community. A home suffered minor damage with windows blown out and debris thrown into trees. More trees were snapped. The tornado weakened and continued east-northeast across Pond Creek to Ashley Road where it damaged a mobile home. The tornado then lifted just to the east-northeast and the damage path ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across portions of West Tennessee during the morning on January 10, 2008. Some storms became severe producing flash flooding, large hail, and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding across northeastern portions of Madison County and northeastern sections of Jackson. Several roadways were covered by up to 2 feet of water. Windy City Road and Bells Highway had to be closed for a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from Northern Arkansas into Southern Missouri during the evening hours of February 5th, 2008. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported 4 inches of snow accumulation across the higher elevations of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Conditions continued to improve across the Central Tennessee Valley in February, with most of the area at least chipping away at the long-term drought conditions. Normal for the month is between four and six inches, so rainfall was slightly below normal in most spots.||Light to moderate rain fell on several days during the first week of the month.  Rainfall events for the rest of the month were generally light (less than one inch per event).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the Country Farms subdivision on Schaeffer Road in Eads. One home had minor damage to the roof, gutters and a gazebo. A few small trees in the area were uprooted or snapped. The tornado then traveled northeast and caused the former Eads Elementary School to lose a significant amount of roof shingles. The tornado then crossed Collierville-Arlington Road and snapped and uprooted several trees near the Cypress Creek Canal. The tornado then tracked into Fayette County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from Northern Arkansas into Southern Missouri during the evening hours of February 5th, 2008. Supercells developed out ahead of the system during the late afternoon into the early evening. The storms produced tornadoes, large hail and damaging winds. As the supercells moved east during the evening, a cold front moved into West Tennessee. A squall line developed along the front and produced another round of large hail and damaging winds. The front continued to push east into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down at the Union University Campus near the U.S. Highway 45 Bypass in Jackson producing tremendous damage. Fifty one people were injured at Union University as two dormitories collapsed during the passage of the violent tornado. Eighty percent of the dorms were severely damaged or destroyed. Three academic buildings received major damage and 14 other campus buildings were damaged. Several hundred vehicles in the campus parking lots were also damaged or destroyed. The damage in the University area was rated EF-4 with maximum winds of 170 mph. The track was 125 yards wide. The damage at the university alone was estimated at $40,000,000. The tornado then tracked across the U.S. Highway 45 Bypass damaging several banks and businesses before hitting the Regional Hospital and adjacent doctors' offices. The tornado then moved across North Jackson inflicting significant damage to many subdivisions and businesses. The Chapel Creek, Indian Hills and Wyndhurst subdivisions were all struck by the tornado. The tornado also damaged the Jackson Oaks Senior Living Complex. The tornado continued to track northeast striking the Northside High School in the Oak Hill area. The tornado then moved through the Walnut Trace subdivision before crossing Old Medina Road and damaging more houses. The tornado then continued northeast causing EF-3 damage near the intersection of Christmasville Road and Ashport Road in Northeast Madison County before lifting. The Madison County Fire Station No. 11 was destroyed. The station's fire engine and pumper, water and brush trucks were all damaged. Several homes in the area were also damaged. The maximum winds were 145 mph in this area and the track was 100 yards wide. Along the track of this tornado over 70 structures were destroyed with over 500 more damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from Northern Arkansas into Southern Missouri during the evening hours of February 5th, 2008. The front continued to push east into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed into Madison County from Haywood County, Tennessee. The tornado struck the town of Huntersville and produced EF-3 damage. One home was totally destroyed on Huntersville-Denmark Road. One person was killed inside the home. Another home was destroyed in Huntersville. One person was killed in this home as well. Numerous homes were destroyed or damaged along the path in Huntersville area. The St. John No. 2 Baptist Church was severely damaged and the church bus was flipped upside down and tossed several hundred feet. The tornado lifted northeast of Huntersville near Interstate 40. A rest area along Interstate 40 was severely damaged. Thirteen tractor-trailers and several cars were also blown off of the Interstate due to the tornado. One car was tossed 75 yards from Interstate 40 into a farm field. Along the track of this tornado over 50 structures were destroyed and over 80 more were damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed into Haywood County from Fayette County. The tornado produced EF-2 damage in Dancyville. One home was destroyed and another suffered severe damage. A church also had major damage and numerous large trees were uprooted. The tornado continued northeast and caused destruction in Hillville. Two mobile homes were totally destroyed and one house had major damage. A convenience store also suffered roof damage. In addition, an outbuilding was completely flattened and large trees were uprooted or snapped. Several other homes had minor damage along the path. The tornado continued northeast eventually crossing into Madison County. Cleanup costs incurred by Haywood County totaled $168,980.

Supercells developed out ahead of the system during the late afternoon into the early evening. The storms produced tornadoes, large hail and damaging winds. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The initial tornado touchdown occurred west of the Tennessee River near the intersection of Coffee Landing Road and Towboat Lane about 740 pm CST. The tornado then quickly raced northeast causing extensive damage to homes and trees just east of the Tennessee River in the Oak Grove, Cerro Gordo and Swift Communities. The tornado destroyed 59 houses, 11 mobile homes, 11 public buildings, a vacant store and 11 farm buildings. Another 117 structures received varying degrees of damage. Four of the 11 public buildings belonged to the Sharon Baptist Church. The church lost its sanctuary, preschool, academy and student center, off Cravens Road in the Oak Grove area. Two of the five injuries occurred at the church while two more injuries occurred in homes in the Oak Grove area. All three deaths occurred in mobile homes. Two of the deaths occurred in the Cerro Gordo area while the third fatality occurred in northeast Hardin County in the vicinity of Nance Bend Road and Highway 128. The tornado continued northeast before lifting near the intersection of Highway 114 and Highway 128 around 757 pm CST. The path length was about 16 miles and the maximum width was one half mile. The highest estimated winds were around 170 mph. Utility restoration costs were estimated at around $200,000.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed into Henry County from Weakley County, Tennessee. The tornado traveled northeast damaging an abandoned home and several trees on Bethany Road as well as a barn. On Hayes Store Road, a home suffered severe shingle damage and damage to the soffits and trimmings. Five huge oak trees in front of the home were either uprooted or broken off. Two barns at this location also received damage. Several more trees were snapped off across the street. Further northeast on Wiggins-Capps Road, a barn was destroyed, along with several snapped trees. Three more homes were damaged on Highway 69. The first home sustained shingle damage with a large antenna being knocked over by the tornado. The second home lost several shutters with also a large antenna and flag pole being knocked over. The third home lost half of the roof and a barn was destroyed behind the house. At the end of the tornado track, 3 mobile homes suffered damage with two being severe. The first mobile home had roof damage. The other two mobile homes were double-wides and were sitting on blocks. Both shifted about six feet off their blocks. A barn and a 26 X 26 ft. shop building were completely destroyed. The tornado lifted along Jones Mills Crossland Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front tracked through the region on the 6th.  A squall line developed ahead of it and pushed through east Tennessee during the morning hours.  Numerous damage reports from thunderstorm winds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marion County highway department reported numerous trees down across the north half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front tracked through the region on the 6th.  A squall line developed ahead of it and pushed through east Tennessee during the morning hours.  Numerous damage reports from thunderstorm winds were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported several trees and powerlines down in Maryville.  Alcoa Electric reported 1100 to 1200 customers in Maryville lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient brought non-thunderstorm damaging winds to the higher elevations of east Tennessee on the 17th.  Strong winds in the valley was responsible for one death in Loudon County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Park officials reported several trees downed by non-thunderstorm winds across the Foothills Parkway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from Northern Arkansas into Southern Missouri during the evening hours of February 5th, 2008. Supercells developed out ahead of the system during the late afternoon into the early evening. The storms produced tornadoes, large hail and damaging winds. As the supercells moved east during the evening, a cold front moved into West Tennessee. A squall line developed along the front and produced another round of large hail and damaging winds. The front continued to push east into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked trees across roads in the southern tip of Decatur County.

A 100-year-old oak tree was blown down on Polk St. Another tree was blown down on Lincoln St. In addition, a tree fell on a house on Lindenwood Street in Tullahoma, and also just 4 houses away, a tree fell on a 2007 Dodge Grand Caravan.||1,300 people were without power in Tullahoma at one time during the thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were a few hail and strong straight line wind events on June 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn was destroyed and trees were blown down. Newspaper story said the barn was worth $12,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One severe weather event on June 12, 2008 in Montgomery County and June 13, in White Co.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree fell at 335 Academy Avenue in strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonable freezing temperatures were observed for several hours during the early morning of the 16th. Minimum temperatures in rural locations dipped into the 28 to 32 degree range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dipped to around 32 degrees for up to three hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were estimated by trained spotter to be 60 mph. Also, trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down one quarter mile east of Brunswick Road interchange along Highway 385 in the Salem Estates Subdivision. The tornado tracked non-continuously northeast to the Tipton County line, near the Collierville-Arlington Road. Damage sustained in the Salem Estates subdivision was rated an EF-0. One roof of a home was partially removed along with several blown out windows. In addition, some minor tree damage occurred in this area. Further northeast, along Moose Road west of Beaver Creek, the tornado intensified and produced EF-2 damage. Significant tree damage occurred from the tornado in this area. In addition, two power line support towers were knocked down and one was twisted. Significant damage occurred further northeast along Osborntown Road as extensive tree damage was observed. Large trees were snapped and uprooted. In addition, two large well constructed new homes were heavily damaged. One home had more than half of its roof removed along with all windows blown out. Brick was also dislodged from portions of the structure. The second home received extensive damage with the entire second story being removed from the tornado. Inside walls were compromised and debris was deposited inside the home and in the front yard. One minor injury occurred from this segment of EF-2 rated damage. Additional damage occurred further northeast across Macedonia Road producing additional tree damage and minor damage to one home and one mobile home. Damage also occurred just south of where the tornado crossed into Tipton County where five homes sustained light to moderate damage along Collierville-Arlington Road. This damage has been rated EF-1. One of the homes sustained significant damage to portions of the roof and adjoining levels. The other four homes had damage to the roofs and windows. Large trees, some up to 3 feet in diameter were snapped and uprooted. The tornado then tracked into Tipton County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stretched from Middle Tennessee through North Mississippi into South Central Arkansas during the evening hours of March 14, 2008. Showers and thunderstorms developed along and north of the front including portions of West Tennessee. Some of the storms became severe during the overnight hours of March 14th producing large hail, damaging winds and some localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail covering ground in the Rockford Subdivision near the intersection of Old Brownsville Road and Ellendale Road.

Showers and thunderstorms developed over West Tennessee in association with the system. Some of the stronger storms produced flash flooding and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees on New Castle Road and Hickory Valley Road.

A low pressure system developed along the Gulf of Mexico and tracked northeastward into the Southeastern United States. An abundance of moisture was associated with the system. In addition, cold air was also in place over West Tennessee as a result heavy snow fell in association with the system. Snowfall totals of 3 to 7 inches were seen across West Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of three to five inches fell in Selmer, Adamsville and surrounding areas. Numerous accidents were reported from the snow-covered roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado continued northeast from DeSoto County, Mississippi. After the tornado crossed into Tennessee, several warehouses sustained damage. The DSC warehouse near Clarke Road was struck causing EF-2 damage. Three people were killed inside the warehouse. Hardy Bottling Company sustained substantial damage as well. Tractor-trailers were tossed about in the area. Windows were shattered in the Willow Lake Business Park and utility poles and trees were snapped in the area. A utility substation sustained severe damage along Clarke Road causing major power outages in the area. The tornado then continued northeast and struck homes along Maple Tree Drive and Pinbranch Court. Five homes received major damage and had to be condemned. Other homes sustained roof damage. The tornado continued northeast and struck the Hickory Ridge Mall causing a wall to collapse at the Sears Department Store. Six injuries occurred at the mall. This damage was estimated at EF-1. The tornado then struck a Taco Bell along Winchester Road before lifting. Thirteen people in all were transported to an area hospital due to injuries sustained from the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds produced roof damage to at least 10 homes in the Quail Ridge area outside of Trenton. Trees were uprooted and a gazebo damaged as well in the area. In the downtown court square of Trenton, roof damage occurred to buildings and windows were broken from the wind. A tree fell on a vehicle at a home on High Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm struck Middle Tennessee producing 4 to 12 inches of snow in the Western Part of Middle Tennessee and 1 to 4 inches elsewhere. There was a combination of freezing rain and sleet during the beginning of this winter storm event. The county that had the most snow was Houston County with 12 inches, and snow drifts ranging from 2 to 3 feet. This was confirmed by two different observers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of sleet, snow and freezing rain hit the county. Snow accumulations were about 1 to 3 inches across the county by 10 AM CST Sat. March 8. Driving was treacherous due to the snow and ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Super Severe Weather Outbreak on Feb. 5, 2008 produced supercelluar thunderstorms, well in advance of a multicell line of thunderstorms. The whole episode lasted about 6 hours. This occurred ironically while many states, including Tennessee, were participating in the Super Tuesday Primary Election. Fortunately, polls had already closed in the mid state when these tornadoes struck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirteen people were killed, and 44 folks were injured. Most of the fatalities occurred in Williams, just northwest of Lafayette. Also, 170 homes were destroyed, and 9 homes had major damage. ||There was one indirect fatality, a male 48 years of age, due to carbon monoxide poisoning in his home from a generator on Feb. 6, 2008.||Pres. George W. Bush toured Macon County destruction on February 8th and promised relief to individuals. ||The logging industry in Macon County was hit hard. About a million dollars worth of trees were destroyed, which will take decades to replenish. It was estimated that the tornado outbreak did 10 million dollars worth of damage to trees in the state of Tennessee.||One large brick home worth about 350 thousand dollars was poorly built. The bricks rested on cinder blocks that were not cemented. This was noticed on a storm survey.||Schools were closed for over a week, and classes did not start again until Tuesday, February 19.||FEMA spent 4.1 million dollars for housing displaced residents in trailers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from Northern Arkansas into Southern Missouri during the evening hours of February 5th, 2008. Supercells developed out ahead of the system during the late afternoon into the early evening. The storms produced tornadoes, large hail and damaging winds. As the supercells moved east during the evening, a cold front moved into West Tennessee. A squall line developed along the front and produced another round of large hail and damaging winds. The front continued to push east into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-2 tornado touchdown occurred in Spring Creek along Highway 152 about one quarter mile west of Highway 70. One home suffered total roof loss and a partial failure of the front exterior wall. Three additional homes received extensive roof damage. Large trees were snapped and uprooted. Maximum winds were estimated at 125 mph with a maximum width of 150 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty gradient winds caused a fatality as a man was trying to hold a mattress in the back of a truck. The man was in the bed of a city truck trying to hold down a mattress. The mattress was lifted by a wind gust and flipped the man upside down up into the air. He came down and hit his head on the blacktop. The maximum wind gust recorded at Nashville International Airport was 47 mph at 1018 CST. The incident occurred in Mt. Pleasant near the Kitty Hawk Manufacturing on State Route 243. It is against state law to ride in the bed of a truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Super Severe Weather Outbreak on Feb. 5, 2008 produced supercelluar thunderstorms, well in advance of a multicell line of thunderstorms. The whole episode lasted about 6 hours. This occurred ironically while many states, including Tennessee, were participating in the Super Tuesday Primary Election. Fortunately, polls had already closed in the mid state when these tornadoes struck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in Perry County. Across extreme northwest Lewis County, trailers were destroyed. Roofs were damaged. Barns and outbuildings were flattened along McCord Hollow Road and Cane Creek Rd. This tornado continued into Lewis and Hickman Counties.||TEMA reported this tornado destroyed 4 homes, and one mobile home in Perry County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe weather activity started in the western part of Middle Tennessee Friday evening. The activity became tornadic early Saturday morning on May 3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pavilion was destroyed at the Montgomery County Fairgrounds, which is about 2 miles south-southwest of downtown Clarksville. Telephone poles were snapped in half. A wooden utility pole was leaning. ||There was damage to the bleachers at the fairgrounds. ||Fairground bleachers were deemed unsafe and closed by Clarksville Park and Recreation officials.||Fencing was blown away along the top bleachers. ||Clarksville Park and Recreation officials said that there were cracks in the bleacher's cinder block supports. They also found that portions of the structure had moved at least 2 inches by the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north over the Midsouth during the late evening hours of April 3, 2008. Some of the storms produced flash flooding, damaging winds, and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding along Highway 51 in Covington as a stream overflowed its bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northeast from eastern Kansas into the Great Lakes region and rapidly deepened.  Numerous strong to severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a strong trailing cold front which pushed in southern middle Tennessee during the afternoon hours and into northwestern Georgia after midnight. Some of these severe storms produced hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms produced winds in excess of 60 mph in the Huntland community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall during the month of March into the beginning of April brought rivers and their tributaries to high levels. Some rivers came out their banks and flooded many roadways. A few deaths as a result of the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 56 year old tow truck driver was one of two drivers who arrived to help a man who had driven into flood waters off of Holmes Road near the intersection of Reynolds Road after falling asleep at the wheel. The tow truck driver tied an air hose around his waist for safety and climbed onto the car to attach the cable from the tow truck. The car began to float. The water forced the car through the culvert into the raging Nonconnah Creek tributary, with the force of the current snapping the hose that tethered the man to the tow truck. The car sank 75 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system moved into the Midsouth during the evening hours of April 10, 2008 and continued moving through the area during the day on April 11, 2008. The system first produced a few severe storms during the early morning hours of April 10, 2008 over Northeast Arkansas as it approached the area. These storms quickly died off. However by the late afternoon and evening hours, redevelopment occurred across much of the Midsouth. These storms produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding. The storms continued into the overnight hours before dying off. Redevelopment then occurred across Northeast Mississippi during the afternoon hours of April 11, 2008 as the system departed the region.

A cold front approached the Midsouth during the afternoon hours of May 2nd, 2008, Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead and along the front. Storms quickly became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding, and a few tornadoes. The severe weather continued into the early morning hours of May 3rd, 2008 with the passage of the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked trees and power lines down along Highway 114 outside of Clarksburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved north as a warm front during the day of May 10, 2008 as a strong upper level trough moved into the Midsouth. Behind the warm front, the atmosphere became very unstable which allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some of the thunderstorms quickly became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding into the early morning hours of May 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HAM radio operators reported 60 mph wind gusts near the intersection of Shelby Drive and Swinnea. Five power poles were snapped on Shelby Drive. A tree fell on a car on Cullenwood Avenue in Whitehaven and a person was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with several upper level disturbances that quickly moved through northwest upper level flow. The thunderstorms quickly became severe due to daytime heating especially during the afternoon and evening hours. The thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and locally heavy rain which produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail between quarter and half dollar size fell along Highway 79, northeast of Atwood to near Trezevant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with several upper level disturbances that quickly moved through northwest upper level flow. The thunderstorms quickly became severe due to daytime heating especially during the afternoon and evening hours. The thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and locally heavy rain which produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Reagan. State Highway 100 and several secondary roads were closed with up to a foot of water covering them. Some of the roads were damaged as a result of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Organized band of storms shifted southeast from Eastern Kentucky and Southwestern Virginia into Northeast Tennessee. Other storms developed in the moist and unstable environment present across Central East Tennessee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe convection developed ahead of a surface low near the Ohio River|along the Indiana/Kentucky border with quasi-stationary frontal boundary extending east into the Mid Atlantic region. A mid level impulse and upper level cold pocket contributed to moderate instability for storm formation. The event produced mostly damaging winds and a limited amount of hail production.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down on Brookside Drive behind Indian Path Hospital. Structural damage also occurred at the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions /D2/ were maintained during the month of July.||Rains were of the typical summertime spotty variety.  Most areas received at least two inches for the month, with a few spots including Winchester receiving four inches. Rivers, still feeling the effects of the 2007 drought, dropped to base-flow quicker than usual following rainfalls and there was not yet enough groundwater recharge to raise base-flow permanently.

Severe drought conditions /D2/ were maintained during the month of July.||Rains were of the typical summertime spotty variety. A weak area of convergence extending southwest from an area of low pressure in the New England area pushed southeast through northern Alabama by midnight on the 31st.  Very moist low level moisture ahead of this convergence combined with dry air aloft to produce isolated  microburst wind damage near interstate 65 in Southern Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were downed by thunderstorm winds on Hughey road.

A cold front tracked across east Tennessee during the afternoon hours producing scattered thunderstorms along it.  A few storms became severe with hail up to golfball-size reported, as well as damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Daily Post-Athenian newspaper in Athens reported the Athens Utility Board reported that thunderstorm winds downed a few trees and powerlines across mainly the eastern portions the county.  Over 1,700 customers lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave produced temperatures above 100 degrees on July 29th with upper 90 degree temperatures on July 30th, and 31st. The temperatures combined with high humidity produced heat index values of over 100 degrees during this time frame as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four heat-related deaths occurred as a result of the heat wave at the end of July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually dry weather continued in September especially across the east half of Middle Tennessee.  Many of these locations received less than one-half inch of rain in September or roughly 2-4 inches LESS than the amount that typically occurs in September.  Sparse rainfall combined with temperatures of about 2 degrees above average helped intensify the on-going drought and spread it slightly westward (deeper into Middle TN).

Tropical Storm Ike moved across Northeast Arkansas into Southern Missouri. The storm weakened to a tropical depression as it moved. Tropical storm force winds occurred over Northwest Tennessee as a result. Substantial damage occurred from downed trees and power lines. Some structural damage occurred as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from Tropical Depression Ike affected Obion County. The Union City AWOS reported sustained winds of 24 knots between 700 am LST and 800 am  before losing power from the storm. The AWOS reported a peak wind of 44 knots. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down across the county. The most pronounced damage occurred north of a line from South Fulton to Union City to Troy. A total of eleven homes were damaged with two of those homes sustaining major damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Ike moved across Northeast Arkansas into Southern Missouri. The storm weakened to a tropical depression as it moved. Tropical storm force winds occurred over Northwest Tennessee as a result. Substantial damage occurred from downed trees and power lines. Some structural damage occurred as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from Tropical Depression Ike affected Dyer County. The Dyersburg AWOS had sustained winds of 25 to 31 knots between 700 am LST and 900 am LST. The AWOS reported a peak wind gust of 45 knots. Numerous trees...power lines...and signs were knocked down across the county. Northern portions of the county was particularly hit hard. One house southwest of Finley sustained partial roof damage. A wildfire started from a power line that was knocked down from the winds. The fire quickly spread across North Dyersburg. No damage was reported from the fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Ike moved across Northeast Arkansas into Southern Missouri. The storm weakened to a tropical depression as it moved. Tropical storm force winds occurred over Northwest Tennessee as a result. Substantial damage occurred from downed trees and power lines. Some structural damage occurred as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from Tropical Depression Ike affected Lake County. Sustained winds were estimated between 25 and 33 knots. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down across the county. A mobile home was destroyed near Tiptonville. The roof off of two classrooms at the Margaret Newton Grade School was blown off. Another building was blown off of it's foundation in Tiptonville. Two buildings were completely destroyed and two businesses had the facade blown off from the winds. Awnings were damaged as well. One house had a tree fall onto it's roof.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds from Tropical Depression Ike affected Weakley County. Winds were sustained between 20 and 30 knots. Numerous trees and power lines were knocked down. In addition, one house in Dresden and one in Martin had a tree fall on their roofs. One carport was damaged. One home in Palmersville sustained brick damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Fay brought heavy rain to much of West Tennessee during the late afternoon of August 25th, 2008. The heavy rain produced flash flooding over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August finally marked the first substantial rainfall for the Central Tennessee Valley since we entered into the heart of the drought in early 2006. The month began rather uneventfully with a series of mainly dry cold fronts. The parade of cold fronts was stopped just after mid-month as Tropical Storm Fay approached Florida. No measurable rain was observed between the 13th and 23rd. The big rainmaker was Tropical Storm Fay, which brought between two and six inches of rain, the heaviest across Lincoln County.  For most of the Tennessee Valley, this was the first substantially above-normal rainfall month of 2008.||River base-flow actually increased as a result of Fay, the first sign of improvement in stream-flows from the drought. Deep groundwater also recovered to near average, and soil moisture reached near average levels for the first time in about two years.||Drought conditions improved to below severe drought /D2/ status in Lincoln and Moore Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved to below the /D2/ Severe category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary moved from northwest to southeast and sparked scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across southern middle Tennessee. There were scattered reports of large hail and damaging winds in portions of southern Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in the Charity community, six miles north of Lynchburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed by thunderstorm winds five miles southeast of Huntland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large tree limbs were blown down by thunderstorm winds in the Charity community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August finally marked the first substantial rainfall for the Central Tennessee Valley since we entered into the heart of the drought in early 2006. No measurable rain was observed between the 13th and 23rd. The big rainmaker was Tropical Storm Fay, which brought between two and six inches of rain, the heaviest across Lincoln County.  For most of the Tennessee Valley, this was the first substantially above-normal rainfall month of 2008.||River base-flow actually increased as a result of Fay, the first sign of improvement in stream-flows from the drought. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions have improved to below the /D2/ Severe category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extensive rains fell in western Middle Tennessee in October but much less rain occurred where it was needed most in the east half of the region, including the Cumberland Plateau.  Rains across the east half of Middle Tennessee were roughly one inch below normal which added to the on-going rainfall deficit across the area.  Much of the east part of Middle Tennessee remained in severe (D2 classification) drought conditions during October.

Rains across the east half of Middle Tennessee were roughly one inch below normal which added to the on-going rainfall deficit across the area.  Much of the east part of Middle Tennessee remained in severe (D2 classification) drought conditions during October.
A cluster of thunderstorms produced severe weather (mainly branches and felled trees) across the northern part of Middle Tennessee Wednesday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were down 4 miles northeast of Hartswville at Highway 10 near Highway 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With unstable conditions present, scattered severe thunderstorms initiated during the afternoon hours on the 9th and continued into the early morning  hours of the 10th.  Storm reports contained both damaging thunderstorm winds and hail up to golfball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported occasional pea to penny-size hail near Glenwood School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With unstable conditions present, scattered severe thunderstorms initiated during the afternoon hours on the 9th and continued into the early morning  hours of the 10th.  Storm reports contained both damaging thunderstorm winds and hail up to golfball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Monroe County Highway Department reported several trees downed by thunderstorm winds on roadways east of Madisonville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A boundary across east Tennessee triggered isolated thunderstorms during the early morning hours.  A few storms became severe with damaging thunderstorm winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported a few trees downed by thunderstorm winds in the Robbins area near Huntsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked into east Tennessee triggering scattered severe thunderstorms during the evening hours.  The storm reports were for damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported several trees downed by thunderstorm winds across the southwest portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front tracked into east Tennessee triggering scattered severe thunderstorms during the evening hours.  The storm reports were for damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported numerous trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds countywide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary from a large thunderstorm complex set off showers and thunderstorms over the Midsouth during the afternoon and evening hours of May 26, 2008. Some of the activity lingered into the morning hours of May 27th. The storms produced damaging winds, large hail, and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew trees and power lines down across Brownsville. A few trees were reported down on Highway 76. In addition, trees were reported down near Haywood High School. Trees were also knocked down on numerous houses. One man was injured when a tree fell on his house. A roof was blown off the Dynametal building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated pulse convection developed in a weakly unstable atmosphere on the Cumberland Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Diurnal severe thunderstorms developed along a boundary during the afternoon and evening hours.  Most of the storm reports were for thunderstorm wind damage, but a few hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway department reported numerous trees, limbs and debris downed by thunderstorm winds east of Tazewell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong upper low pressure system in addition to a cold front moved into West Tennessee during the afternoon and evening hours of January 8, 2008. Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the system. Some of the storms became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds, flash flooding and isolated tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down trees blocking Holladay Road in Northwest Decatur County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were six confirmed tornadoes on April 11, 2008. Large hail and straight line winds were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees blew down. One tree fell on a house on Arrowhead Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure system moved northeast from Middle Tennessee into Southeast Kentucky with line of thunderstorms developing just ahead of front. Instability was low so convection was low topped. Most of the wind damage in this event was straight line in character. However, closer to surface low, the environment was more conducive to the development of rotating storms and an EF1 tornado touched down at Big Stone Gap in Southwest Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three power poles were snapped and downed by strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving line of severe convection coincident with very strong cold front passed across the mid south and entered the MRX warning area late in the evening resulting in widespread wind damage across Southwest Virginia, East Tennessee, and Southwest North Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down in the Clairfield area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Squall line type thunderstorms, with some bow echoes, occurred on January, 10. Isolated supercells spawned a tornado in Wayne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported at the intersection of Wilson Pike and Clovercroft Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from Northern Arkansas into Southern Missouri during the evening hours of February 5th, 2008. Supercells developed out ahead of the system during the late afternoon into the early evening. The storms produced tornadoes, large hail and damaging winds. As the supercells moved east during the evening, a cold front moved into West Tennessee. A squall line developed along the front and produced another round of large hail and damaging winds. The front continued to push east into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked a few trees down in Greenfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked from Northern Arkansas into Southern Missouri during the evening hours of February 5th, 2008. Supercells developed out ahead of the system during the late afternoon into the early evening. The storms produced tornadoes, large hail and damaging winds. As the supercells moved east during the evening, a cold front moved into West Tennessee. A squall line developed along the front and produced another round of large hail and damaging winds. The front continued to push east into the overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down a couple of power poles in McKenzie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-cursor to a major severe weather outbreak on April 11 with storms on April 10, 2008.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across portions of West Tennessee during the morning on January 10, 2008. As the front lifted north, surface low pressure tracked along the Tennessee-Mississippi border during the afternoon hours. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the system. Some storms became severe producing flash flooding, large hail, and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew a roof off of a barn near Acton. A few trees were also knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved north as a warm front during the day of May 10, 2008 as a strong upper level trough moved into the Midsouth. Behind the warm front, the atmosphere became very unstable which allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some of the thunderstorms quickly became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding into the early morning hours of May 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell along Highway 59 in Mason.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across portions of West Tennessee during the morning on January 10, 2008. As the front lifted north, surface low pressure tracked along the Tennessee-Mississippi border during the afternoon hours. Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with the system. Some storms became severe producing flash flooding, large hail, and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in East Memphis. Many side streets were closed as a result.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe weather events occurred on July 29th and 30th ahead of a cold  |front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike destroyed the communications system, phone system, computers and cameras at the sheriff's office and other Wilson County buildings. The Nave funeral home lost its carport to the lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms occurred on July 21st and July 22nd ahead and along an approaching cold front. The front brought cooler air and temperatures into the 80s from July 23 to July 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Homestead Circle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in association with several upper level disturbances that quickly moved through northwest upper level flow. The thunderstorms quickly became severe due to daytime heating especially during the afternoon and evening hours. The thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and locally heavy rain which produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Jacks Creek. State Highways 22A and 100 East were flooded with several feet of water as well as many secondary roads. Roby Road was washed out as a result and a culvert had to be replaced. A mudslide occurred on Highway 100, 10 miles east of Henderson near the area known as Jones Cut Hill. Several fields were flooded as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong polar vortex was situated over the Eastern Great Lakes and Southeastern Canada with strong unidirectional flow out of the northwest.  Instability was marginal but capes managed to top out around 1000 J/Kg and with moderate to strong shear, severe thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the late afternoon and early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down in the national forest near Oswald Dome Road damaging trees inside the national forest. The tornado path was approximately 25 yards wide and 2.1 miles long and ended near highway 30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of instability present with cold front lifting scattered thunderstorms in the vicinity of interstate 40 and points farther south. The storms produced a large amount of hail around one inch in diameter and downed numerous trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell at McGhee Tyson Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Local Atmosphere became strongly unstable during the afternoon and evening and severe convection erupted in this environment ahead of an outflow boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Chattanooga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Super Severe Weather Outbreak on Feb. 5, 2008 produced supercelluar thunderstorms, well in advance of a multicell line of thunderstorms. The whole episode lasted about 6 hours. This occurred ironically while many states, including Tennessee, were participating in the Super Tuesday Primary Election. Fortunately, polls had already closed in the mid state when these tornadoes struck.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across extreme northwest Lewis County, trailers were destroyed. Roofs were damaged. Barns and outbuildings were flattened along McCord Hollow Road and Cane Creek Rd.||TEMA reported 6 homes had major damage, one mobile home was destroyed in Lewis County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front moved north as a warm front during the day of May 10, 2008 as a strong upper level trough moved into the Midsouth. Behind the warm front, the atmosphere became very unstable which allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop. Some of the thunderstorms quickly became severe and produced large hail, damaging winds and flash flooding into the early morning hours of May 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew three trees down near the intersection of Vildo Road and Highway 100.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of January 29, 2008, severe thunderstorms occurred ahead of an approaching cold front. Once the thunderstorms moved out of the area, strong gradient winds took hold and produced some non-thunderstorm wind damage across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down around Hartsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of January 29, 2008, severe thunderstorms occurred ahead of an approaching cold front. Once the thunderstorms moved out of the area, strong gradient winds took hold and produced some non-thunderstorm wind damage across Middle Tennessee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WEMA reported quarter size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power lines were reported down on Virtue rd.

The thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and locally heavy rain which produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds blew a few trees down in Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and evening hours.  Storm reported contained both damaging winds and large hail.  Hail reports were as large as baseball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported several trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds along Pea Ridge Road to the Walland area.
